questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman undergoes elective right hemicolectomy for colon cancer. On day 3 post-operatively, she has a distended abdomen, absent bowel sounds, and has not passed flatus or stool. She complains of nausea but no pain. Her vital signs are normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Small bowel obstruction due to adhesions
questions.explanation: Classic presentation: absent bowel sounds, distension, no pain, normal vitals. Small bowel obstruction usually has colicky pain and hyperactive bowel sounds early. Anastomotic leak and abscess typically cause pain, fever, tachycardia. Ogilvie's shows marked colonic distension with caecal dilation, often after non-abdominal surgery.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man undergoes total hip replacement. On day 2, his abdomen is distended, he has not passed flatus, and bowel sounds are absent. He has no pain. An abdominal XR shows gas throughout the small and large bowel with mild colonic dilation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: After orthopaedic surgery, ileus is common. AXR shows diffuse gas in both small and large bowel, typical of ileus. Ogilvie's would show isolated colonic dilation with a cutoff at the splenic flexure. Mechanical obstruction has air-fluid levels and paucity of distal gas. Constipation and urinary retention do not cause absent bowel sounds.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman complains of persistent hiccups and nausea 24 hours after uneventful laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She has mild abdominal distension and has not yet passed flatus. Bowel sounds are reduced. She has no shoulder tip pain or fever. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hiccups are a classic sign of gastric distension due to ileus. Absence of shoulder tip pain and fever makes diaphragmatic irritation or bile leak less likely. Pancreatitis would have epigastric pain and raised amylase. Opioid-induced nausea does not explain distension and reduced bowel sounds.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with Crohn's disease undergoes a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy for terminal ileal stricture. On day 3, she has abdominal distension, vomiting, and has not passed flatus. She has no abdominal pain. Temperature is 37.1°C, heart rate 80, BP 110/70. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Recurrent Crohn's stricture at the new terminal ileum
questions.explanation: Absence of pain and normal vitals argue against obstruction (colicky pain) or abscess. Anastomotic stricture and recurrence typically present later. Post-operative ileus is common and expected at this time.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man undergoes emergency Hartmann's procedure for perforated diverticulitis. He has a history of heart failure and is on multiple medications. On day 4, he has a distended abdomen, no bowel sounds, and has not passed flatus. He is afebrile with stable vitals. His potassium is 3.3, but otherwise normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Small bowel obstruction due to postoperative adhesions
questions.explanation: Absence of pain, fever, and normal vitals favour ileus over obstruction (colicky pain), abscess (pain and fever), C. difficile (diarrhoea, fever), or Ogilvie's (massive colonic dilation). The hypokalaemia is mild and common in post-op patients, not the primary cause.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman is day 3 after a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy for colon cancer. She has not opened her bowels since surgery, feels nauseous, and has a distended but non-tender abdomen. Observations are stable. Which is the most appropriate first investigation to diagnose post-operative ileus?
questions.explanation: Abdominal X-ray is the first-line imaging to assess bowel gas pattern and exclude mechanical obstruction in suspected ileus. CT is reserved for cases with suspicion of complication or obstruction; blood tests may support but do not confirm the diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is day 5 after an open repair of a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm. He has abdominal distension, vomiting, and has not passed flatus or faeces. An abdominal X-ray shows dilated loops of both small and large bowel with no transition point or air-fluid levels. What is the most likely interpretation?
questions.explanation: Generalized dilation of both small and large bowel without a transition point or air-fluid levels is classic for paralytic ileus. Mechanical obstruction typically shows a transition point and proximal dilation only; pseudo-obstruction usually involves just the colon.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is day 2 after an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She complains of bloating and has passed flatus only once in the last 24 hours. Which of the following criteria is most commonly used to diagnose post-operative ileus in clinical practice?
options: Absence of flatus for more than 72 hours post-operatively
questions.explanation: The standard definition of postoperative ileus is the failure to pass flatus or stool for >72 hours after surgery. Abdominal distension and loss of bowel sounds are common but not specific; NG tube use is a management response, not a diagnostic criterion.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man is day 4 after an emergency laparotomy for perforated duodenal ulcer. He has a distended abdomen, high nasogastric tube output, and has not passed flatus. An abdominal X-ray shows multiple air-fluid levels in the small bowel with no colonic gas. What is the most appropriate next investigation to differentiate paralytic ileus from mechanical small bowel obstruction?
options: CT abdomen with intravenous and oral contrast
questions.explanation: CT with oral and IV contrast is the gold standard to identify a transition point and confirm mechanical obstruction. Angiography is for ischaemia; contrast enema assesses the colon; repeat X-ray is less accurate; lactate may suggest ischaemia but does not differentiate.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with an end colostomy is day 1 after a total abdominal hysterectomy. She has had no stoma output and feels bloated. Observations are normal. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm a diagnosis of post-operative ileus in this patient?
questions.explanation: Abdominal X-ray is simple and can show dilated bowel loops consistent with ileus. CT is more detailed but not first line; irrigation is therapeutic; amylase checks for pancreatitis; sigmoidoscopy is unnecessary.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman undergoes elective right hemicolectomy for colon cancer. On day 1 post-op, she is stable, has mild abdominal distension, but no nausea or vomiting. She has not passed flatus. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Nil by mouth and intravenous fluids
questions.explanation: First-line management of uncomplicated postoperative ileus is conservative: nil by mouth, IV fluids, and mobilisation. Nasogastric tubes are reserved for vomiting or severe distension; prokinetics have limited evidence and are not first-line; early oral diet may worsen distension.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man develops postoperative ileus after emergency repair of perforated duodenal ulcer. He has persistent nausea and has vomited twice. Which antiemetic is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Cyclizine has anticholinergic properties that can exacerbate ileus by reducing gastrointestinal motility. Other antiemetics like ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist) or metoclopramide (prokinetic) are preferable.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman undergoes total hip replacement under spinal anaesthesia. Three days later, she develops severe abdominal distension, no flatus, and a plain abdominal radiograph shows caecal diameter of 11 cm. She has no peritoneal signs. What is the first-line pharmacological therapy?
questions.explanation: This is acute colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie's syndrome). First-line pharmacological therapy is neostigmine (2 mg IV) after exclusion of mechanical obstruction and contraindications (bradycardia, asthma).

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with asthma is 4 days post-laparoscopic cholecystectomy. He has postoperative ileus with abdominal distension. In managing his ileus, which medication is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Neostigmine is a parasympathomimetic that can cause bronchospasm and is contraindicated in asthma. Other prokinetics like metoclopramide and domperidone are safer, though evidence for ileus is limited.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman undergoes emergency caesarean section. On day 2, she has abdominal distension and has not passed flatus. She is on morphine patient-controlled analgesia. Which change to her analgesic regimen is most appropriate to help resolve her ileus?
options: Switch to oral paracetamol and ibuprofen
questions.explanation: Opioids exacerbate ileus. Switching to non‑opioid analgesics (paracetamol and NSAIDs) reduces opioid exposure and may hasten resolution. Adding opioids or switching to another strong opioid would not help.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman on post-operative day 2 following an uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy has mild abdominal distension but has passed flatus. She is haemodynamically stable, tolerating sips of water, and denies vomiting. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Insert a nasogastric tube and aspirate
Urgently refer to the surgical registrar
Encourage mobilisation and oral intake as tolerated
questions.explanation: Mild ileus is expected after laparoscopic cholecystectomy; conservative measures like mobilisation and encouraging oral intake are first-line. NG tube, imaging, and antiemetics are not required in this stable, passing-flatus patient.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man on post-operative day 3 after an open right hemicolectomy for colon cancer has abdominal distension, nausea, and has vomited once. He has not opened his bowels. Vital signs: HR 80, BP 130/80, temp 37.1°C. Bowel sounds are absent. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Insert a nasogastric tube and apply free drainage
Keep nil by mouth and start IV fluids
Administer a single dose of metoclopramide 10mg IV
Request an urgent CT abdomen with IV contrast
Escalate to the surgical registrar for possible return to theatre
questions.explanation: This is a typical post-operative ileus with vomiting; the first step is supportive care with NBM and IV fluids. NG tube is indicated if vomiting persists or distension is severe; CT is for suspected obstruction or leak; metoclopramide is not recommended for ileus.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman on post-operative day 5 after an emergency laparotomy for perforated diverticulitis (Hartmann's procedure) develops sudden-onset severe abdominal pain, distension, vomiting, and fever (38.5°C). HR 110, BP 90/60, abdomen rigid with rebound tenderness. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start intravenous fluid resuscitation with a bolus of 500mL Hartmann's
Insert a nasogastric tube and commence free drainage
Organise an urgent CT abdomen with IV contrast
questions.explanation: Peritonitic signs and haemodynamic instability suggest an anastomotic leak or intra-abdominal catastrophe requiring immediate surgical assessment; although fluid resuscitation and antibiotics are needed, the priority is escalating to the surgical team. CT should not delay definitive care.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman on post-operative day 4 after a total abdominal hysterectomy has progressive abdominal distension, obstipation, and mild discomfort but no vomiting or fever. An abdominal X-ray shows dilated small bowel loops with multiple air-fluid levels. She had a previous laparoscopy for adhesive small bowel obstruction 2 years ago. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to the on-call surgical team for emergency surgery
Start conservative management with NBM and NG tube
Arrange a CT abdomen with oral and IV contrast
Request a colonoscopy to exclude mechanical obstruction
questions.explanation: In a patient with suspected adhesive small bowel obstruction without signs of strangulation, a water-soluble contrast challenge is diagnostic and therapeutic; it can predict need for surgery and reduce length of stay. CT is reasonable but not the immediate next step, and colonoscopy is not indicated.

questions.stem: An 85-year-old man on post-operative day 3 after a hemiarthroplasty for hip fracture has abdominal distension and constipation for 2 days. He has Parkinson's disease and takes levodopa. Vital signs: HR 90, BP 140/80, temp 36.8°C. He has not vomited and passed flatus yesterday. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Review and optimise his Parkinson's medication regimen
Request an urgent CT abdomen with oral contrast
Administer a phosphate enema or glycerin suppository
Urgently refer to the surgical team for assessment
Encourage early mobilisation and oral fluids
questions.explanation: Drug-related ileus is common in Parkinson's disease; worsening of parkinsonism or anticholinergic side effects from medications can cause constipation and distension. Adjusting Parkinson's medications should be the first step before more invasive investigations or treatments.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman is 3 days post elective right hemicolectomy. She has had minimal flatus and mild abdominal distension. Over the past 2 hours she develops increasing abdominal pain and a temperature of 38.5°C. On examination, the abdomen is distended and tender. Which single clinical finding most strongly suggests a surgical complication rather than simple post-operative ileus?
options: Nasogastric output of 600 mL over 4 hours
Guarding and rigidity in the right lower quadrant
questions.explanation: Localised peritonism (guarding/rigidity) indicates peritoneal irritation, likely from an anastomotic leak or intra-abdominal collection, and requires urgent CT or surgical exploration. Simple ileus would not produce peritonism.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man is 4 days post open repair of an infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm. He has persistent abdominal distension and has not passed flatus. He has had several episodes of vomiting. Which single feature of the vomiting should alert you to a possible small bowel obstruction rather than ileus?
questions.explanation: Feculent vomiting suggests stasis and bacterial overgrowth in the obstructed small bowel, which is a red flag for mechanical obstruction. Simple ileus typically produces non-feculent gastric or bilious vomitus.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman is 2 days post laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She reports mild nausea and has not passed flatus. She suddenly becomes tachycardic (heart rate 120 bpm) and hypotensive (blood pressure 80/50 mmHg). Abdominal examination reveals mild distension but no obvious peritonism. Which single investigation finding most strongly suggests a life-threatening complication requiring emergency intervention?
options: Free gas under both diaphragms on erect chest X-ray
Right upper quadrant tenderness on deep palpation
questions.explanation: Free intraperitoneal gas in a shocked post-laparoscopic patient indicates a perforated viscus (e.g., bile duct injury or duodenal perforation). This is a surgical emergency. Mild free gas can be normal post-laparoscopy, but significant volume with shock is ominous.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman is 6 days post left hemicolectomy. She has had a prolonged ileus with minimal flatus. Over the past 24 hours she develops progressive abdominal distension and respiratory distress. An abdominal X-ray shows a dilated colon. Which radiological finding mandates immediate decompression to prevent perforation?
options: Multiple air-fluid levels in the small bowel
Loss of haustrations in the descending colon
Distended sigmoid colon with air-fluid level
questions.explanation: A cecal diameter >9-10 cm in acute colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie's syndrome) indicates high risk of perforation. This requires urgent endoscopic or radiological decompression. Smaller diameters are often observed.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man is 4 days post emergency laparotomy for a perforated duodenal ulcer. He has persistent ileus with a high nasogastric output. He becomes confused, tachycardic (heart rate 115 bpm), and hypotensive (blood pressure 85/50 mmHg). Blood tests are taken. Which single laboratory value is most indicative of a critical complication requiring immediate action?
questions.explanation: A raised lactate in this setting with shock suggests mesenteric ischaemia, perforation, or severe hypoperfusion, which demands urgent laparotomy. Other values are deranged but are not the immediate critical finding.
